_note: Loops. Curriculum sequence follows Foundations of Python Programming (Runestone) ch.7 and Think Python ch.7. Both are copyleft (GFDL / CC BY-NC-SA), so NO content is copied from either: only the topic order is used, and a topic sequence is not copyrightable. Every question below is original. Concepts covered: for over a sequence, range bounds and the off-by-one, the accumulator pattern, while conditions, the infinite loop, and loop-variable scope after the loop ends.

questions.title: How many times does the body run?

python.prompt: How many lines does this print?
python.explanation: `range(3)` produces 0, 1, 2. That is three numbers, so the body runs three times. The loop variable `i` is not used here, which is fine: sometimes you only want the repetition.

java.prompt: How many lines does this print?
java.explanation: `i` starts at 0 and the loop continues while `i < 3`, so the body runs for i = 0, 1, 2. That is three times. Reading a `for` header left to right (start, keep going while, do after each pass) tells you the count.

javascript.prompt: How many lines does this print?
javascript.explanation: `i` starts at 0 and the loop runs while `i < 3`, so the body runs for i = 0, 1, 2. Three times. Reading a `for` header left to right (start, keep going while, do after each pass) tells you the count.

cpp.prompt: How many lines does this print?
cpp.explanation: `i` starts at 0 and the loop continues while `i < 3`, so the body runs for i = 0, 1, 2. Three times. Reading a `for` header left to right (start, keep going while, do after each pass) tells you the count.

python.prompt: What is the last number printed?
python.explanation: `range(4)` gives 0, 1, 2, 3. It stops just before 4 and never reaches it. If you picked 4, you have met the off-by-one error, which is the most common counting bug there is. To get 1 through 4, write `range(1, 5)`.

java.prompt: What is the last number printed?
java.explanation: The condition is `i < 4`, so when `i` reaches 4 the loop stops without running the body. The last value printed is 3. If you picked 4, you have met the off-by-one error. Changing `<` to `<=` would print 4 as well.

javascript.prompt: What is the last number printed?
javascript.explanation: The condition is `i < 4`, so when `i` becomes 4 the loop stops before running the body again. The last number printed is 3. If you picked 4, you have met the off-by-one error. Using `<=` would print 4 too.

cpp.prompt: What is the last number printed?
cpp.explanation: The condition is `i < 4`, so when `i` reaches 4 the loop stops without running the body. The last value printed is 3. If you picked 4, you have met the off-by-one error. Using `<=` would print 4 as well.

python.code:
total = 0
for price in [4, 7, 2]:
    total = total + price
print(total)
python.explanation: This is the accumulator pattern: start a total at 0, then add to it on every pass. 0 + 4 is 4, plus 7 is 11, plus 2 is 13. Almost every problem that asks you to sum, count, or collect is this pattern in disguise.

java.explanation: This is the accumulator pattern: start a total at 0, then add to it on every pass. 0 + 4 is 4, plus 7 is 11, plus 2 is 13. Almost every problem that asks you to sum, count, or collect is this pattern in disguise.

javascript.explanation: This is the accumulator pattern: start a total at 0, then add to it on every pass. 0 + 4 is 4, plus 7 is 11, plus 2 is 13. Almost every problem that asks you to sum, count, or collect is this pattern in disguise.

cpp.explanation: This is the accumulator pattern: start a total at 0, then add to it on every pass. 0 + 4 is 4, plus 7 is 11, plus 2 is 13. Almost every problem that asks you to sum, count, or collect is this pattern in disguise.

python.code:
for price in [4, 7, 2]:
    total = 0
    total = total + price
print(total)
python.explanation: Look at where `total = 0` sits. It is inside the loop, so it runs again on every pass and wipes out everything added so far. The last pass sets it to 0 and adds 2, so 2 prints. The accumulator has to be created before the loop, not inside it. This one runs without any error, which is what makes it dangerous.

java.explanation: The `total = 0;` inside the loop runs on every pass and wipes out everything added so far. The last pass resets it and adds 2, so 2 prints. The reset belongs outside the loop. Nothing here crashes, which is exactly what makes this bug hard to spot.

javascript.explanation: The `total = 0;` inside the loop runs on every pass and throws away everything added so far. The final pass resets it and adds 2, so 2 prints. The reset belongs outside the loop. Nothing crashes here, which is what makes the bug easy to miss.

cpp.explanation: The `total = 0;` inside the loop runs on every pass and wipes out everything added so far. The last pass resets it and adds 2, so 2 prints. The reset belongs outside the loop. Nothing crashes, which is what makes this bug hard to find.

questions.title: A loop with no way out

python.explanation: Nothing inside the loop ever changes `count`, so `count <= 3` stays true and the loop never ends. Every `while` loop needs something in its body that eventually makes the condition false. When a run of yours times out, this is the first thing to check.

java.explanation: Nothing in the body ever changes `count`, so `count <= 3` stays true and the loop never ends. Every `while` loop needs something inside it that eventually makes the condition false. When a run times out, look here first.

javascript.explanation: Nothing in the body ever changes `count`, so `count <= 3` stays true forever. Every `while` loop needs something inside it that eventually makes the condition false. When a run times out, this is the first thing to check.

cpp.explanation: Nothing in the body ever changes `count`, so `count <= 3` stays true and the loop never ends. Every `while` loop needs something inside it that eventually makes the condition false. When a run times out, look here first.

python.code:
n = 3
while n > 0:
    print(n)
    n = n - 1
python.explanation: The condition is checked before each pass. It prints 3, 2, 1, and when `n` reaches 0 the check `n > 0` fails, so the body does not run again. 0 is never printed.

java.explanation: The condition is checked before each pass. It prints 3, 2, 1, and once `n` hits 0 the check `n > 0` fails, so the body does not run again. 0 is never printed.

javascript.explanation: The condition is checked before each pass. It prints 3, 2, 1, and once `n` reaches 0 the check `n > 0` fails, so the body does not run again. 0 is never printed.

cpp.explanation: The condition is checked before each pass. It prints 3, 2, 1, and once `n` reaches 0 the check `n > 0` fails, so the body does not run again. 0 is never printed.

python.explanation: The inner loop runs all the way through for every single pass of the outer one. Three outer passes, four inner passes each, so 3 times 4 is 12. Nested loops multiply, they do not add. That is why a nested loop over a big list gets slow so fast.

java.explanation: The inner loop runs all the way through for every pass of the outer one. Three outer passes, four inner passes each, so 3 times 4 is 12. Nested loops multiply, they do not add. That is why a nested loop over a big array gets slow so fast.

javascript.explanation: The inner loop runs all the way through for every pass of the outer one. Three outer passes, four inner passes each, so 3 times 4 is 12. Nested loops multiply, they do not add. That is why a nested loop over a big array gets slow so fast.

cpp.explanation: The inner loop runs all the way through for every pass of the outer one. Three outer passes, four inner passes each, so 3 times 4 is 12. Nested loops multiply, they do not add. That is why a nested loop over a big array gets slow so fast.

questions.title: Inside the loop, or after it?

python.code:
total = 0
for n in [1, 2, 3]:
    total = total + n
    print(total)
python.prompt: How many lines does this print, and what is the last one?
python.explanation: The `print` is indented, so it sits inside the loop and runs on every pass: 1, then 3, then 6. Three lines. Un-indent that `print` and you get a single line showing 6. Whether a line is inside or after the loop decides whether you get a running report or one final answer, and both are useful. Decide which you want.

java.prompt: How many lines does this print, and what is the last one?
java.explanation: The `println` is inside the braces, so it runs on every pass: 1, then 3, then 6. Three lines. Move it below the closing brace and you get one line showing 6. Whether a statement is inside or after the loop decides whether you get a running report or one final answer.

javascript.prompt: How many lines does this print, and what is the last one?
javascript.explanation: The `console.log` is inside the braces, so it runs on every pass: 1, then 3, then 6. Three lines. Move it below the closing brace and you get one line showing 6. Inside or after the loop decides whether you see a running report or one final answer.

cpp.prompt: How many lines does this print, and what is the last one?
cpp.explanation: The `cout` is inside the braces, so it runs on every pass: 1, then 3, then 6. Three lines. Move it below the closing brace and you get one line showing 6. Inside or after the loop decides whether you see a running report or one final answer.

questions.title: Counting from somewhere other than zero

python.explanation: With two arguments, `range(start, stop)` begins at the start and stops just before the stop. So it gives 2, 3, 4, 5. The end is always excluded, which is the same rule as `range(4)` stopping at 3.

java.explanation: The header sets `i` to 2 and keeps going while `i < 6`, so it prints 2, 3, 4, 5 and stops. The first part of a `for` header is where you start, and the second is where you stop, so you can begin anywhere you like.

javascript.explanation: The header starts `i` at 2 and keeps going while `i < 6`, so it prints 2, 3, 4, 5. The first part of the header is where you start and the second is where you stop, so you can begin anywhere.

cpp.explanation: The header starts `i` at 2 and keeps going while `i < 6`, so it prints 2, 3, 4, 5 and stops. The first part of the header is where you start and the second is where you stop, so you can begin anywhere.

python.explanation: This is the counter pattern, which is the accumulator with an `if` in front of it. Walk the list and check each one: 45 no, 82 yes, 91 yes, 60 no, 77 yes. Three qualify. Notice the counter starts at 0 before the loop, and the `if` decides whether this pass adds anything.

java.explanation: This is the counter pattern, which is the accumulator with an `if` in front of it. Check each one: 45 no, 82 yes, 91 yes, 60 no, 77 yes. Three qualify. The counter starts at 0 before the loop, and the `if` decides whether a pass adds anything.

javascript.explanation: This is the counter pattern, which is the accumulator with an `if` in front of it. Check each one: 45 no, 82 yes, 91 yes, 60 no, 77 yes. Three qualify. The counter starts at 0 before the loop, and the `if` decides whether a pass adds anything.

cpp.explanation: This is the counter pattern, which is the accumulator with an `if` in front of it. Check each one: 45 no, 82 yes, 91 yes, 60 no, 77 yes. Three qualify. The counter starts at 0 before the loop, and the `if` decides whether a pass adds anything.
